claim: The austenite grain size of 0.04Nb steel is approximately half that of 0.02Nb steel due to the precipitation of NbC particles.
evidence_text: The austenite grain size of 0.04Nb steels is approximately half that of 0.02Nb steels due to the precipitation of NbC particles.

claim: The actual measured chemical composition of the 0.02Nb steel is Mn 12.80, Al 9.01, Cr 3.01, C 1.39, Nb 0.021 wt% with Fe balance; the 0.04Nb steel composition is Mn 12.82, Al 9.12, Cr 2.99, C 1.40, Nb 0.039 wt% with Fe balance.
evidence_text: Table 1. Chemical compositions (wt%) of the studied low-density steel strips. Samples Mn Al Cr C Nb Fe 0.02Nb steel 12.80 9.01 3.01 1.39 0.021 Bal. 0.04Nb steel 12.82 9.12 2.99 1.40 0.039 Bal.

claim: XRD patterns show that the microstructure of all Nb-containing steels consists of ferrite and austenite, and the diffraction peak intensities of both phases exhibit almost no significant change after aging at different temperatures.
evidence_text: Figure 1displays the XRD patterns of the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, in which the microstructure of all the Nb-containing steels consists of ferrite and austenite. After aging treatments at different temperatures, the diffraction peak intensity of both austenite and ferrite in the 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels shows almost no significant change, as depicted in Figure 1a,b.

claim: Irregular-shaped ferrite is randomly dispersed in the austenite matrix; the ferrite volume fraction in as-cast 0.02Nb steel is ≈16.2 vol% and after aging at 723, 773, 823 K for 3 h is 16.4, 17.1, and 18.3 vol%, respectively; for 0.04Nb steel the corresponding fractions are 16.3, 17.2, 18.4, and 18.1 vol%, indicating that ferrite content is not affected by aging temperature.
evidence_text: The ferrite content in the as-cast 0.02Nb steel is measured as ≈16.2 vol%. Following aging treatment at 723, 773, and 823 K for 3 h, the ferrite content in the steels is 16.4, 17.1, and 18.3 vol%, respectively. Similarly, the 0.04Nb steels under the same aging states have ferrite contents of 16.3, 17.2, 18.4, and 18.1 vol%, respectively. These results indicate that the ferrite contents in the low-density steels are not affected by the aging temperature.

claim: Nb addition leads to the formation of submicron white particles within the austenite matrix, and TEM selected area electron diffraction and EDS analysis identify these particles as NbC.
evidence_text: the Nb addition leads to the formation of submicron white particles within the austenite matrix, as indicated by the purple arrows in Figure 2. Figure 3 shows the TEM selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis and corresponding EDS analysis of the white particles in as-cast 0.02Nb and 0.04Nb steels, in which the white particles are identified as NbC.

claim: Both the size and volume fraction of NbC particles increase slightly with higher aging temperature and higher Nb content, according to statistical image analysis.
evidence_text: According to the statistical results shown in Figure 4, both the size and volume fraction of the NbC particles increase slightly with higher aging temperature and more Nb content.

claim: Tensile tests were conducted at ambient temperature with a strain rate of 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ on specimens having a gauge width of 4 mm and gauge length of 12 mm, with at least four repetitions per steel strip.
evidence_text: The tensile property tests were carried out using a MTS Criterion 44 universal testing machine at ambient temperature. The strain rate during the tests was set as 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The test specimens had a gauge width of 4 mm and a gauge length of 12 mm to ensure consistent measurements. To ensure accurate data analysis, each steel strip underwent a minimum of four repetitions during the testing process.

claim: Spin-polarized DFT calculations using VASP with PBE-GGA, PAW potentials, a 500 eV plane-wave cutoff, Gaussian smearing of 0.05 eV, GGA+U (U = 2.59 eV for Fe), a vacuum spacing of 15 Å, and 6×6×6 Monkhorst–Pack k-point sampling were employed to study the influence of Nb on κ-carbide formation.
evidence_text: To explore the influence of Nb addition on the formation of κ-carbide, this study employed spin-polarized density functional theory (DFT) calculations within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) utilizing the first-principles Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) recipe, which was implemented in Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP). The ion nuclei were characterized by projected enhanced wave (PAW) potentials, while the valence electrons were treated with a plane-wave basis set and a kinetic energy cutoff of 500 eV. To accurately compensate for the partial occupation of the Kohn–Sham orbitals, the Gaussian smearing approach was utilized with a width of 0.05 eV. The convergence of the geometry optimization process was determined by ensuring that the energy change was below 0.03 eV Å−1. The U scheme with a value of 2.59 eV was applied for Fe atoms. The slab structure had a vacuum spacing of 15 Å in the direction perpendicular to the structure plane. Brillouin zone integration of the structure was achieved using 6×6×6 Monkhorst–Pack k-point sampling.
